To find the equilibrium population we set the rate of change to zero and solve for P using the given parameters where r equals 0.1 and K equals 1000 while h(t) varies between 5 and 15. The equation becomes 0 = 0.1P(1 - P/1000) - (10 + 5sin(pi/6 t)). Since h(t) fluctuates there is no single constant equilibrium value but rather a range of quasi-equilibrium states depending on the season. If we assume an average harvesting rate of 10 turtles per year the equilibrium P_e solves 0.1P(1 - P/1000) = 10 which yields P_e approximately 118 or 882 turtles. For the second part we substitute K(t) = 1000 - 3t into the equilibrium condition 0 = rP(1 - P/K(t)) - h_avg. We use the average harvest of 10 to simplify the stability analysis as the sine term averages to zero over time. The quadratic equation for P becomes 0.1P - 0.1P^2/(1000 - 3t) - 10 = 0. Multiplying by the denominator gives 0.1P(1000 - 3t) - 0.1P^2 - 10(1000 - 3t) = 0. This simplifies to -0.1P^2 + (100 - 0.3t)P - (10000 - 30t) = 0. Real positive solutions for P exist only if the discriminant is non-negative. The discriminant D equals (100 - 0.3t)^2 - 4(-0.1)(-10000 + 30t). Expanding this we get 10000 - 60t + 0.09t^2 - 4000 + 12t = 0.09t^2 - 48t + 6000. Setting D = 0 to find the tipping point yields t = (48 +/- sqrt(2304 - 2160)) / 0.18.
